metadata.subject: The Great Wave off Kanagawa — a towering cresting wave with foamy claws looming over small fishing boats, Mount Fuji small and serene in the background, rendered in the ukiyo-e woodblock print style with deep Prussian blue and white

painting_plan.subject: The Great Wave off Kanagawa — a towering cresting wave with foamy claws looming over small fishing boats, Mount Fuji small and serene in the background, rendered in the ukiyo-e woodblock print style with deep Prussian blue and white
painting_plan.expanded_subject: A dramatic seascape dominated by a massive cresting wave whose foamy claw-like tips curl menacingly over three small oshiokuri-bune fishing boats struggling in the turbulent sea. Mount Fuji, small, pale, and serene, anchors the composition in the background between the wave's claws. The palette is dominated by Prussian blue (Bokashi) gradients, crisp white foam, and a pale cream sky, rendered in the flat, graphic, highly stylised manner of ukiyo-e woodblock printing.

layers.name: Sky and horizon ground
layers.description: Lay down the entire background sky from the top of the canvas down to roughly y=420. Use a pale cream-white at the top transitioning into a very light cool blue-grey near the horizon. This establishes the luminous, flat sky typical of Hokusai's print. The lower quarter of the canvas (y=420 to y=600) receives a base wash of deep Prussian blue for the sea floor of the composition.
layers.techniques: Broad horizontal wet-brush sweeps across the full canvas width to create smooth, flat colour bands. Subtle gradient achieved by overlapping slightly darker strokes near the horizon. Keep strokes even and parallel, mimicking the flat colour blocks of woodblock printing.
layers.shapes: Wide horizontal bands spanning the full 800px width. Gentle curved horizon line at approximately y=400-420.
layers.highlights: The sky should feel luminous and open — nearly white at the top, cooling and slightly deepening toward the horizon. No texture in the sky; flatness is essential to the ukiyo-e aesthetic. The deep blue base at the bottom anchors the composition and will be built upon in later layers.

layers.name: Mount Fuji and distant sea
layers.description: Paint the iconic silhouette of Mount Fuji small and serene in the upper-centre-left background, approximately centred around x=310, y=280, with its peak at around y=200 and base width of about 120px. Its slopes are pale blue-white with a snow cap of near-white. Also paint the distant flat sea bands between the horizon and the base of the main wave — horizontal striped bands of graduated Prussian blue suggesting receding water in the middle distance (y=380 to y=460).
layers.techniques: Mount Fuji rendered with clean, precise outline strokes forming its triangular silhouette. Fill with flat pale blue-white wash. Snow cap applied with near-white dry-brush. Distant sea painted with tightly spaced horizontal line strokes of alternating Prussian blue tones, creating the characteristic striped water pattern of Hokusai.
layers.shapes: Isoceles triangle for Fuji. Thin, closely spaced horizontal parallel lines for the distant sea surface, each line slightly curved to suggest gentle swells.
layers.highlights: Fuji must appear small, calm, and almost ghostly — a counterpoint to the chaos of the wave. Its pale tone should make it recede. The distant sea stripes should be fine and regular, growing slightly wider and darker as they approach the foreground, reinforcing depth.

layers.name: Great Wave main body
layers.description: Paint the massive body of the Great Wave — the primary compositional element. The wave rises from the lower-left, arcs dramatically upward to its peak at approximately x=500, y=80, then curls over to the right. The main body occupies roughly x=50 to x=750, y=80 to y=520. Use deep Prussian blue for the dark interior face of the wave and mid-blue for the curved back. The wave's interior concave face is the darkest region. Also paint the secondary smaller wave forms in the lower foreground (y=460 to y=600) as rolling dark blue masses.
layers.techniques: Large sweeping arc strokes to define the curved silhouette of the wave's body. The interior face of the wave painted with broad wet-brush strokes following the curve of the water. Layered arcs of slightly different blue tones build up the sense of mass and volume. The outer back of the wave uses lighter blue. Foreground wave masses use bold polyline strokes to define their rolling forms.
layers.shapes: A dominant large arc curving from lower-left to upper-centre then hooking right — the iconic wave crest shape. Subsidiary rounded mound shapes in the foreground for smaller waves. The overall silhouette echoes a claw or hand reaching over.
layers.highlights: The darkest Prussian blue (#0D2240, #1B3A6B) should dominate the concave interior face of the wave, creating dramatic contrast with the white foam to come. The wave's outer convex back is slightly lighter. Emphasise the sheer scale and mass — this wave should feel overwhelming and monumental.

layers.name: Wave foam claws and spray
layers.description: Add the iconic white foam details that define the Great Wave — the claw-like fingertips of foam at the crest (approximately x=350 to x=700, y=80 to y=200), the white foam streaks running down the interior face of the wave, and the churning white spray at the base where the wave meets the sea. These white elements are the most recognisable feature of the composition. Also add the white foam lines along the backs of the foreground wave masses.
layers.techniques: The claw tips rendered as precise, curved arc strokes forming elongated teardrop or comma shapes — each claw is a cluster of 3-5 curved lines converging to a point. Interior foam streaks applied as flowing curved lines following the wave's curvature downward. Splatter strokes used sparingly for sea spray at the base and around the crest. Dry-brush used for the textured foam along wave edges. Chalk strokes for soft diffuse spray areas.
layers.shapes: Elongated curved claw or talon shapes at the crest — these are the most iconic element. Long sinuous curved lines of foam descending the wave face. Small circular and irregular splatter dots for spray. Thin curved parallel lines along wave ridges.
layers.highlights: The foam claws must be crisp, graphic, and precise — they are the emotional centrepiece of the composition. Pure white against the deep Prussian blue creates maximum contrast. The interior foam streaks should flow naturally with the wave's motion. Avoid overworking — Hokusai's foam is elegant and deliberate, not chaotic. The splatter should be restrained and concentrated at the base and crest.

layers.name: Fishing boats
layers.description: Paint the three small oshiokuri-bune fishing boats being tossed in the wave's trough. Two boats appear in the lower-centre area (approximately x=200 to x=450, y=400 to y=520) and one partially visible at lower-right (x=550 to x=680, y=440 to y=530). Each boat is a long, narrow, low-profile vessel with a slightly upturned bow and stern, dark in colour. Tiny oarsmen figures can be suggested along the sides of the boats. The boats are dwarfed by the surrounding wave.
layers.techniques: Boats drawn with precise, clean polyline strokes defining their elongated hull shapes. The hulls are nearly black-dark blue, rendered as flat silhouettes in the ukiyo-e manner. Oarsmen suggested as tiny repeated vertical line marks along the boat sides. The boats' curvature follows the wave motion — they pitch and toss. Minimal detail — the boats are intentionally small and simple to emphasise the wave's dominance.
layers.shapes: Long, narrow elliptical hull forms with pointed or slightly upturned ends. Thin parallel lines suggesting oars or rowers. The boats angle slightly to follow the wave's slope.
layers.highlights: The boats must read as tiny and vulnerable against the massive wave. Their dark tone anchors them visually but they should not compete with the wave. A thin highlight line of pale blue-white along the upper hull edge suggests reflected light on wet wood. The human figures, though tiny, remind the viewer of the human stakes — keep them gestural and small.

layers.name: Final details, outlines and ukiyo-e refinements
layers.description: Apply the final layer of crisp defining outlines, additional foam detail, and tonal refinements that give the composition its woodblock-print graphic quality. Reinforce the wave's outer contour with a strong dark outline. Add fine line texture to the wave body suggesting water movement. Refine the foam claw edges with precise dark outlines. Add any remaining spray details, strengthen the Fuji outline, and add the subtle blue bokashi gradient shading to the sky if needed. Final small white highlights on wave peaks and boat hulls.
layers.techniques: Fine precise line strokes to reinforce all major contours — wave silhouette, foam claws, boat hulls, Fuji outline. Burn strokes to deepen the darkest shadow areas within the wave interior. Dodge strokes to lift and brighten the foam crest and sky. Fine parallel line hatching on the wave body to suggest turbulent water texture in the ukiyo-e manner. Chalk for soft atmospheric haze around Fuji. Final white line accents on foam edges.
layers.shapes: Crisp contour lines following all major forms. Fine parallel curved lines on wave surface. Small comma and teardrop accent marks for foam. Clean geometric outline for Fuji.
layers.highlights: This layer unifies the entire composition. The strong dark outlines are essential to the woodblock aesthetic — every major form should have a clean, confident edge. The burn tool deepens the wave's interior to near-black at its darkest. Dodge brightens the foam tips to pure white. The overall effect should feel graphic, bold, and precisely controlled — the hallmark of Hokusai's masterwork.

painting_plan.overall_notes: The composition follows Hokusai's original layout closely: the wave dominates the upper two-thirds of the canvas in a dynamic diagonal arc, Fuji sits small and serene in the background gap between the wave's claws, and the boats are dwarfed in the trough below. The Prussian blue palette (introduced to Japan via European pigments around 1820) is the defining colour — use it in at least four tonal values from near-black (#0D2240) to mid-blue (#4A8AC4). White is used only for foam and snow — never as a background fill. The sky's pale cream warmth contrasts with the cool blue of the sea. Flatness and graphic clarity are paramount — avoid blending or painterly softness except in the sky wash. Each stroke should feel deliberate and purposeful, as if carved into a woodblock. The emotional core is the tension between the wave's violent energy and Fuji's eternal stillness.

strokes.batch_reasoning: Starting with 5 broad horizontal wet-brush strokes that establish the fundamental sky-to-sea gradient for Layer 1. The first two strokes lay the warm cream-white sky at the top (y=10 and y=100), the third and fourth introduce the cooling blue-grey transition toward the horizon (y=210 and y=330), and the fifth stroke establishes the deep Prussian blue sea base at the bottom (y=460). Each stroke spans the full 800px canvas width to create the flat, even colour blocks characteristic of Hokusai's ukiyo-e woodblock print aesthetic. The wet-brush type with high softness values ensures smooth, blended transitions between the colour zones without harsh edges.

Five broad horizontal wet-brush sweeps lay the foundational colour structure for Layer 1. The topmost stroke uses warm pale cream (#F5F0DC) to establish the luminous sky top. Moving downward, strokes cool progressively through a warm off-white, then into the light blue-grey transition zone (#C8D8E8), deepening further toward the horizon (#A0B8CC). The final stroke covers the lower sea area with deep Prussian blue (#1B3A6B) using high thickness and opacity to create a strong anchoring base. This mirrors Hokusai's flat, banded colour approach characteristic of ukiyo-e woodblock printing. The wet-brush type ensures soft, blended edges between bands rather than harsh boundaries, appropriate for the sky wash.

This iteration continues building the five core horizontal band structure established in iteration 1. Five wet-brush sweeps span the full canvas width: warm cream at the very top (y=60), cooling cream-beige in the upper-mid sky (y=160), transitioning to cool grey-blue in the mid sky (y=265), deepening to steel blue near the horizon (y=370), and a strong Prussian blue sea base (y=490). The wet-brush softness and flow values create gentle bleed and overlap between bands, smoothing the gradient transitions that were identified as needing refinement. The thick Prussian blue band at the bottom builds saturation for the sea base. This reinforces the flat, luminous ukiyo-e sky aesthetic while deepening the sea foundation.

Iteration 4 continues building the sky-to-sea gradient with five horizontal wet-brush passes targeting key transition zones. The first stroke at y=160 adds a warm grey-cream tone to the upper-mid sky, bridging the cream top and the blue transition. The second at y=260 introduces a cool grey-blue to begin the transition zone. The third at y=350 deepens the blue-grey near the horizon. The fourth and fifth strokes at y=440 and y=540 reinforce the deep Prussian blue sea base with increasing opacity and saturation. All strokes span the full canvas width with gentle horizontal paths to maintain the flat, woodblock-print aesthetic.

This iteration reinforces the four key tonal bands of the sky-to-sea gradient: (1) A luminous warm cream wet-brush pass at y=60 strengthens the pale top sky region; (2) A cool light grey-blue pass at y=200 builds up the mid-sky transition zone; (3) A deeper blue-grey pass at y=330 pushes the lower sky toward the horizon tone; (4) A strong Prussian blue pass at y=460 deepens the sea base wash for saturation and opacity; (5) A dodge stroke centred at the top brightens the uppermost sky region to maintain the luminous, open quality characteristic of Hokusai's ukiyo-e sky. These overlapping wet-brush passes build up flat, even colour bands while the dodge ensures the cream top remains airy and light.

This iteration continues building the sky-to-sea gradient established in previous passes. The first three wet-brush strokes target the critical transition zone (y=250-370) with intermediate cool blue-grey tones (#C0CCDA, #A8BCCC, #8AAAC0) at moderate opacity to smooth the gradient from cream sky to the horizon — this is the key area needing refinement per the strategy. The fourth wet-brush stroke reinforces the Prussian blue sea base at y=480 with higher opacity to deepen saturation in the lower region. The dodge stroke at the top brightens the cream sky area to maintain the luminous, open quality characteristic of Hokusai's sky — ensuring the top reads as nearly white and radiant against the cooler lower tones.

This iteration continues building the sky-to-sea gradient established in previous passes. Stroke 1 adds a medium cool blue-grey band in the transition zone around y=290 to smooth the cream-to-blue shift. Stroke 2 deepens the transition further at y=350 with a slightly darker blue-grey, reinforcing the gradient's lower portion. Strokes 3 and 4 add additional saturated Prussian blue passes at y=460 and y=540 to push the sea base toward full opacity and depth — the deep Prussian blue (#1B3A6B and #152E58) needs multiple overlapping passes to achieve the rich, flat saturation characteristic of woodblock printing. Stroke 5 is a dodge at the very top centre to lift the cream region back toward luminous brightness, counteracting any darkening from previous passes and ensuring the sky reads as open and light at the top.

This batch of 5 strokes continues the systematic development of Layer 1. This is iteration 15, the final required iteration for Layer 1. The five strokes serve as finishing passes: (1) A wet-brush smoothing pass at y=350 with medium blue-grey #6A8E9F further resolves the sky-to-horizon gradient transition. (2) A wet-brush pass at y=390 with darker #5A7E8F sharpens the lower sky boundary just above the horizon line. (5) A final dodge at the top-centre maintains the luminous, near-white cream quality of the sky apex, essential to Hokusai's open, bright sky aesthetic. Together these finalise the layer.

This first batch begins Layer 2 by establishing the two key elements: Mount Fuji and the distant sea bands. The first two wet-brush strokes lay down Fuji's pale triangular body centred around x=310 with peak near y=200 and base at y=340, using pale blue-white tones (#D0DCE8, #E8EEF4) that will make it appear ghostly and receding against the sky gradient already on canvas. The third stroke — a dry-brush — begins the snow cap at the peak with a near-white tone (#F4F2EC), giving it the characteristic Hokusai snow detail. The final two strokes place the first horizontal sea band lines at y=400-416 using mid-Prussian blue tones, beginning the characteristic striped water pattern of the distant sea. These are deliberately light first passes — subsequent iterations will refine and build upon all these elements.

This iteration begins establishing the two primary objectives of Layer 2. This iteration continues building Fuji's pale triangular silhouette with two overlapping wet-brush passes — the first broader and slightly cooler (#C8D8E8) to establish the mountain body volume, the second narrower and slightly warmer (#D8E4EE) to reinforce the central spine of the triangle. The chalk stroke at the peak reinforces the near-white snow cap with grainy texture reminiscent of woodblock print grain. The final sea band at y=472 in near-black (#0D2240) begins the darker foreground transition of the stripe pattern.

This batch continues building Layer 2 systematically. Stroke 1 is a soft wet-brush pass over Fuji's triangular form following the slope path from base-left through peak to base-right, adding another translucent layer of pale blue-grey to solidify the ghostly silhouette without overpowering it. Strokes 2 and 3 add two more horizontal sea band lines at y=476 and y=482 in progressively deeper Prussian blue, continuing the characteristic Hokusai striped water pattern toward the foreground. Stroke 4 applies chalk texture to the snow cap area at the peak (around y=198-205) for the soft grainy quality of near-white snow against pale sky. Stroke 5 is a gentle burn on Fuji's lower-right slope to introduce subtle shadow definition, making the mountain feel three-dimensional while remaining ghostly — consistent with Hokusai's treatment of Fuji as a serene, slightly mysterious presence.

This batch continues the systematic development of Layer 2's two objectives. Two more deep Prussian blue sea band lines at y=488 and y=494 extend the graduated stripe pattern, growing progressively darker as they approach the foreground — reinforcing the sense of receding depth characteristic of Hokusai's water rendering. Finally, a dodge near Fuji's peak lifts the sky brightness slightly, creating the subtle luminous halo that appears in Hokusai's original around the mountain's upper form.

This is the final iteration (15) of Layer 2. Five finishing strokes complete the layer: (1) a soft wet-brush unifying pass over Fuji's triangular body ensures the ghostly pale blue-white silhouette reads cohesively against the sky background; (2) a chalk pass refines the snow cap at the peak with grainy near-white texture characteristic of Hokusai's woodblock print quality; (3) and (4) two crisp pale-blue line strokes reinforce the left and right slope outlines of Fuji's triangular silhouette, giving it the clean graphic edge essential to ukiyo-e style; (5) a final dodge brightens the sky immediately around Fuji's peak, creating the luminous halo effect seen in Hokusai's original that makes the mountain feel serene and otherworldly. Layer 2 is now complete — all objectives met: sea stripe pattern graduated from y=380 to foreground, Fuji silhouette established with snow cap and slope definition.

This first batch of Layer 3 strokes establishes the massive foundational body of the Great Wave. The two large wet-brush sweeps follow the iconic arc trajectory from lower-left rising to the peak at approximately x=500, y=85, using the darkest Prussian blue (#0D2240, #1B3A6B) to create the dark concave interior face of the wave. A broad arc stroke reinforces the overall curved silhouette of the wave form. A horizontal wet-brush pass at y=460-500 begins establishing the foreground wave mass. A second arc in mid-blue (#2A5298) adds the slightly lighter outer back of the wave. Together these strokes lay down the primary compositional mass — the overwhelming dark blue body that will be refined and detailed in subsequent iterations.

The darkest Prussian blue (#0D2240) is used for the main body to establish the deep, dramatic interior face of the wave. A slightly lighter tone (#1B3A6B) follows the same arc to build up tonal depth. Two large arc strokes reinforce the curved silhouette — the darkest for the primary contour, a mid-blue for the outer back differentiation. A horizontal foreground wet-brush pass deepens the lower sea mass where the secondary wave forms will be built. Together these strokes dramatically increase the wave's presence and begin to establish the monumental scale essential to the composition.

This batch focuses on building the fundamental mass and shape of the Great Wave. Stroke 1 is a very thick, dark (#0D2240) wet-brush sweep tracing the full iconic arc from lower-left to upper-right, establishing the wave's primary silhouette with maximum darkness in the concave interior. Stroke 2 repeats the arc path with slightly lighter #1B3A6B to build up layered tonal depth across the wave body. Stroke 3 is a large arc covering the full wave extent, reinforcing the curved outer silhouette of the wave — this defines the iconic shape. Stroke 4 is a broad horizontal wet-brush pass across the mid-section (y≈250) building up the dark interior face of the wave where it curves inward. Stroke 5 is a bold polyline at y≈490-540 establishing the rolling foreground secondary wave mounds as dark blue masses, grounding the composition.

Iteration 4 focuses on dramatically deepening the wave's body mass. Stroke 1 is a very broad, dark (#0D2240) wet-brush sweep tracing the full iconic arc from lower-left up through the peak and hooking right — this is the primary structural backbone of the Great Wave. Stroke 2 repeats this arc at a slightly different path with #1B3A6B to build tonal layering and reinforce the wave's mass. Stroke 3 is a large arc spanning nearly the full canvas bounding box, using the darkest blue at high thickness to define the wave's outer curved silhouette as a bold graphic shape in the ukiyo-e manner. Stroke 4 targets specifically the concave interior face of the wave (the darkest region) with a very thick, high-flow wet-brush pass in pure #0D2240, building up the deep shadow that will contrast dramatically with the white foam in Layer 4. Stroke 5 is a bold zigzag polyline in the foreground (y=488-540) establishing the rolling secondary wave mounds with the characteristic Hokusai wave pattern — these will be developed further in subsequent iterations.

This batch continues building the massive wave body in Layer 3. Stroke 1 is the heaviest wet-brush sweep in the darkest Prussian blue (#0D2240), tracing the full iconic arc from lower-left up to the peak and hooking right — this builds the primary dark mass of the wave's concave interior face. Stroke 2 is a second major wet-brush arc in slightly lighter #1B3A6B, reinforcing the wave body silhouette and adding tonal depth. Stroke 3 is a large arc in mid-blue (#2A5298) following the outer convex back of the wave — this creates the essential tonal differentiation between the dark interior and the lighter outer back that gives the wave its three-dimensional quality. Stroke 4 is a wet-brush pass in lighter #3A6DB5 along the upper outer back of the wave crest, further differentiating the outer convex surface from the dark interior. Stroke 5 is a bold polyline at y=460-520 representing the rolling foreground secondary wave mounds with their characteristic zigzag profile — a key compositional element of the original Hokusai composition.

This iteration continues building the massive Great Wave body with five targeted strokes: (1) A large wet-brush sweep traces the full iconic arc from lower-left up through the wave's spine to the crest at ~x=680, y=90, using the darkest Prussian blue (#0D2240) to deepen the primary wave mass along its central axis; (2) A massive arc stroke with thick width reinforces the entire outer curved silhouette of the wave from lower-left to upper-right, building the fundamental shape with dark blue (#1B3A6B); (3) A mid-blue (#2A5298) wet-brush sweep along the outer back of the wave adds tonal differentiation — the outer convex surface should be lighter than the dark concave interior; (4) A bold jagged polyline across the full canvas width at y=458-500 builds up the rolling secondary foreground wave mounds, creating the characteristic zigzag wave pattern of Hokusai's sea; (5) A burn pass centred on the concave interior face of the wave (x=280, y=300) deepens the shadow zone to near-black, creating the dramatic dark interior that will contrast powerfully with the white foam to be added in Layer 4.

This iteration continues building the Great Wave's massive dark body. Stroke 1 is a broad wet-brush sweep tracing the full iconic arc from lower-left to upper-right with the darkest #0D2240 Prussian blue, reinforcing the primary wave mass along its spine. Stroke 2 is a large arc covering the full wave silhouette bounding box with deep #1B3A6B blue at high thickness to fill in the wave's body volume. Stroke 5 is a burn pass centered on the wave's concave interior face to deepen the shadow zone to near-black, creating the dramatic contrast that will make the white foam pop in Layer 4.

This iteration focuses on: (1) A dry-brush stroke tracing the full wave arc from lower-left to upper-right, adding Hokusai's characteristic textural water-movement lines to the wave surface with bristle gaps showing the underlying layers; (2) A large wet-brush sweep along the same arc path with the darkest Prussian blue (#0D2240) to deepen the wave's primary mass and interior face; (3) A burn pass centered on the concave interior face region (x=300, y=290) to deepen the shadow zone that will contrast dramatically with the white foam in Layer 4; (4) A dense zigzag polyline at y=460-495 to build up the rolling foreground secondary wave mounds as dark masses; (5) An arc stroke at the top-right defining the hooking curl of the wave crest with mid-blue, reinforcing the iconic curling tip of the wave.

This final iteration of Layer 3 consolidates the wave body with five targeted strokes: (1) A dry-brush pass across the mid-section of the wave face adds Hokusai's characteristic water-movement texture lines following the arc trajectory in #2A5298; (2) A burn pass deepens the concave interior shadow zone at the wave's darkest region around x=280, y=300 for maximum dramatic depth; (3) A wet-brush stroke along the outer convex back at the top-right curl (x=580-760) in lighter #4A8AC4 establishes the luminous lighter rim that will contrast beautifully with the white foam in Layer 4; (4) A dense polyline pass at y=452-500 with near-black #0D2240 reinforces the rolling foreground wave mounds with maximum darkness; (5) A dodge pass at the outermost crest edge (x=660, y=120) brightens the very tip of the wave back to suggest reflected light on the wet surface, preparing the surface for the foam layer. With these strokes, Layer 3 is complete.

Opening Layer 4 with the most critical element: the iconic white foam claw arcs at the wave crest. Five bold white arc strokes are placed across the crest zone (x=320-730, y=70-165), each forming a downward-curving comma shape that mimics Hokusai's famous foam talons. The arcs are spaced across the crest to suggest multiple claw tips reaching downward from the wave's peak. Pure white (#FFFFFF) at high opacity creates maximum contrast against the deep Prussian blue wave body established in Layer 3. The arc bounding boxes are sized to produce elongated, elegant curves rather than tight circles — approximating the teardrop/talon silhouette of Hokusai's foam. Slightly varied sizes and positions across the crest width establish the rhythmic, graphic quality of the woodblock print aesthetic.

This second iteration adds four more primary-scale foam claw arcs at the wave crest, positioned to fill in the gaps between the first iteration's arcs and extend the claw cluster further across the crest line (x=340 to x=690, y=70-155). Each arc sweeps from approximately 195-210 degrees to 345-355 degrees, creating the characteristic downward-curling comma/talon shape of Hokusai's foam claws. A fifth smaller secondary arc is placed beneath the leftmost claw cluster to begin building the layered, clustered foam effect. All strokes are pure white or near-white at high opacity to maximise contrast against the deep Prussian blue wave body. The arcs are sized to feel bold and graphic in the ukiyo-e woodblock manner.

This iteration focuses on the leftmost foam claw cluster, adding tertiary (third-layer) arcs that build the characteristic Hokusai concentric-arc foam claw structure. The three arc strokes create depth within the claw by nesting progressively smaller arcs inside the primary and secondary arcs from iterations 1-2. The line stroke begins the first interior foam streak descending from the claw base down the wave's concave face — this flowing white line suggests foam running down the wave interior, a key Hokusai detail. The chalk stroke softens the transition from claw base to wave face, creating a diffuse spray zone that feels naturalistic without being chaotic. All strokes use pure white or near-white to maintain maximum contrast against the deep Prussian blue wave body.

This first iteration establishes the three fishing boats as dark, low-profile silhouettes in the ukiyo-e manner. Boat 1 (lower-centre-left, y≈468-504) and Boat 2 (lower-centre, y≈420-460) are placed in the wave trough area with their characteristic elongated hull forms — closed polylines with slightly upturned bow and stern ends. Boat 3 (lower-right, x=560-685, y=440-470) is smaller and partially visible. The hulls use near-black Prussian blue (#0D2240) for maximum darkness against the wave, rendered as flat silhouettes consistent with woodblock print aesthetics. Thin pale blue-white (#8AAAC0) highlight lines along the upper hull edges of boats 1 and 3 suggest reflected light on wet wood, a subtle but important detail that gives the vessels dimension without competing with the wave's grandeur.

This iteration builds the solid hull silhouettes for two of the three boats. Opening Layer 6 with the most impactful foundational refinements. The burn stroke deepens the wave's dark interior concave face — essential to the dramatic Prussian blue contrast that defines the original. The dodge stroke brightens the foam crest region, pushing the white foam toward pure luminosity. The large dark arc reinforces the wave's primary outer contour — the woodblock print's defining outline that gives the form its graphic clarity. The polyline traces the wave crest's top edge in deep navy, sharpening the silhouette where wave meets sky. The single fine line begins the parallel hatching texture on the wave face that Hokusai used to suggest turbulent water movement. Together these five strokes establish the graphic boldness of the final layer.

evaluations.feedback: The image presented is heavily blurred and abstract, showing only horizontal bands of color — cream/off-white at the top, transitioning through light gray-blue and pale blue in the middle, to a deep navy/Prussian blue at the bottom. While the color palette does include Prussian blue tones reminiscent of Hokusai's iconic use of Berlin blue (bero-ai) in 'The Great Wave off Kanagawa,' the image itself bears virtually no resemblance to Hokusai's artistic style in any meaningful way. There is no discernible wave form, no foamy claws, no fishing boats, no Mount Fuji, and absolutely no evidence of ukiyo-e woodblock print technique. The horizontal banding could loosely suggest a horizon between sea and sky, but this is an extremely generous interpretation.
evaluations.strengths: The color palette does incorporate deep Prussian blue and lighter blue-white tones, which are characteristic of Hokusai's 'The Great Wave.' The tonal gradient from light to dark blue has a vague atmospheric quality that could loosely evoke a seascape.
evaluations.suggestions: The image needs to be completely reworked to resemble Hokusai's style. Key improvements would include: rendering the iconic cresting wave with detailed foam 'claws,' adding the small fishing boats in the troughs, placing Mount Fuji in the background, incorporating the precise flat graphic linework characteristic of ukiyo-e woodblock printing, adding the bold outlines and flat color fills typical of the style, and eliminating the blurriness entirely in favor of crisp, deliberate mark-making.
